title: BST Insert
problem: Insert value into BST. Left < node, right > node. Return root.
concept: Recursive insertion follows BST property: smaller goes left, larger goes right. Null position = where to insert. Returns root so parent's pointer updates correctly. O(h) time where h = tree height.

title: Level-Order (BFS)
problem: Return values level by level as array of arrays.
concept: BFS using a queue. Key insight: capture queue length at start of each level, process exactly that many nodes. This gives level-by-level grouping. BFS = queue, DFS = stack/recursion.
complexity: O(n) time, O(w) space where w = max level width

title: Climbing Stairs
problem: 1 or 2 stairs per step. How many ways to climb n?
concept: This IS Fibonacci. ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2) because from step n you could have come from n-1 or n-2. DP optimization: only need previous two values, so O(1) space instead of O(n) array.

title: Moving Average Filter
problem: Smooth signal with window k. Return averaged values.
concept: Sliding window applied to audio. Same O(n) technique as Max Subarray Sum K but averaging instead of maximizing. In audio: smooths high-frequency noise. Used in envelope followers, level meters, and AGC (automatic gain control). The window size determines smoothing aggressiveness — larger windows = more smoothing but more latency.
audio_connection: Core DSP operation. Used in envelope followers, level meters, AGC. Window size controls smoothing vs latency tradeoff.

title: Zero-Crossing Rate
problem: Rate signal changes sign. Return crossings / total samples.
concept: Voice activity detection (VAD) feature. Speech has lower ZCR than noise. High ZCR indicates unvoiced fricatives or noise. Used as a simple feature in audio ML — often combined with energy for basic VAD. Low cost O(n) computation makes it ideal for real-time.
audio_connection: VAD feature. Speech ~50-200 crossings/sec. White noise ~high. Used in MFCC pipelines and as ML input feature.

title: Frame Energy
problem: Energy per frame = sum of squared samples per frame.
concept: Energy = sum of squared amplitudes per frame. Non-overlapping frames. Used for: VAD (silent frames have low energy), onset detection (energy spike = new sound), and as ML feature. Squaring amplifies loud parts and diminishes quiet parts.
audio_connection: Fundamental audio feature. Used in VAD, onset detection, MFCC pipelines. Frame-based processing is how ALL audio ML works.

title: Invert Binary Tree
problem: Mirror tree — swap all left/right children.
concept: Recursive swap at every node. Famous interview question (the 'homebrew guy' tweet). Three lines: swap left and right, recurse both. Order doesn't matter since we swap before recursing.

title: Validate BST
problem: Is binary tree a valid BST? Left descendants <, right >.
concept: Pass valid range through recursion. Common mistake: only checking immediate children, not all descendants. Range narrows as you go deeper: left child must be in (parent_min, parent_val), right in (parent_val, parent_max).

title: Coin Change
problem: Fewest coins to make amount. -1 if impossible.
concept: Classic unbounded knapsack DP. dp[i] = min coins for amount i. For each amount, try every coin denomination: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1). Bottom-up avoids recursion overhead. Infinity as default handles impossible amounts.

title: Graph BFS Distance
problem: Shortest distances from start node in adjacency list graph.
concept: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs. Distance = parent distance + 1. Only visit each node once (check if already in distance map). Queue ensures we process closest nodes first.

title: Min Stack
problem: Stack with O(1) push, pop, top, getMin.
concept: Auxiliary stack tracks current minimum. Push to min stack when new value <= current min. Pop from min stack when popped value equals current min. O(1) for all operations by trading space for time.

title: Circular Buffer
problem: Fixed-size ring buffer. write(sample) and read().
concept: Ring buffer is THE fundamental audio data structure. Fixed memory allocation, modulo-based wrapping. Used in every real-time audio system: DAWs, effects processors, communication codecs. Write pointer wraps around; when full, oldest data is overwritten (or blocked). The modulo operation is the key: (pointer + 1) % size.
audio_connection: Core audio infrastructure. Every DAW, codec, and real-time audio system uses ring buffers. Buffer size directly determines latency.

title: Peak Detector
problem: Find peaks: value > threshold AND > both neighbors. Return indices.
concept: Onset detection building block. A peak is a local maximum above a threshold. Used for: beat detection, transient detection, pitch estimation. Threshold prevents noise from triggering false peaks. Skip first and last samples since they can't have both neighbors.
complexity: O(n) time, O(k) space where k = number of peaks
audio_connection: Beat detection, onset detection, pitch tracking. Combined with energy and ZCR for robust onset detection.

title: Convolution (FIR)
problem: Convolve signal with kernel. Output length = sig + kern - 1.
concept: Convolution IS FIR filtering. Signal * kernel = output. Each output sample is the weighted sum of signal samples using kernel weights. This is how reverb, EQ, and FIR filters work mathematically. Output length = input + kernel - 1. In frequency domain: convolution = multiplication (FFT makes this O(n log n) for large kernels).
complexity: O(n*m) time where m = kernel length, O(n+m) space
audio_connection: FIR filtering, reverb (impulse response convolution), EQ. FFT-based convolution is O(n log n) for large kernels.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
problem: Length of longest common subsequence of two strings.
concept: 2D DP. Two cases: characters match (diagonal + 1) or don't (max of skip-from-either). The table fills left-to-right, top-to-bottom. Foundation for diff algorithms, DNA sequence alignment, and version control.

title: Number of Islands
problem: Count islands in grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water).
concept: DFS flood-fill: when you find land, increment count and flood-fill-mark the entire connected island as visited. Grid traversal + DFS = graph search on implicit graph. Top interview question.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor
problem: LCA of two values in a BST.
concept: BST property makes this O(h): if both values less than root, go left. Both greater, go right. Otherwise they split — current node IS the LCA. Iterative, no recursion needed.

title: Topological Sort
problem: Valid topological ordering of DAG (directed acyclic graph).
concept: Kahn's algorithm: process nodes with 0 in-degree first. As you remove a node, decrement neighbors' in-degrees. Newly zero nodes join the queue. Used for: build systems, task scheduling, course prerequisites.

title: RMS Level
problem: RMS per frame = sqrt(mean of squared samples).
concept: RMS (Root Mean Square) is the standard audio level measurement. Unlike peak level, RMS reflects perceived loudness. sqrt(mean(samples²)). Used in level meters, loudness standards (EBU R128), and normalization. dBFS = 20*log10(RMS).
audio_connection: Standard loudness measurement. VU meters, EBU R128 loudness. RMS correlates with perceived loudness better than peak.

title: Noise Gate
problem: If frame RMS < threshold, silence it. Return modified samples.
concept: Noise gate: silence below threshold, pass above. Real-world gates have attack, hold, and release times. This simplified version demonstrates the core logic: measure level per frame, zero out if below threshold. Used in podcasts, live sound, and noise reduction.
audio_connection: Used in every mixing console and DAW. Podcast noise reduction, live sound, drum isolation.

title: Decibel Conversion
problem: ampToDb(amp) = 20*log10(amp). dbToAmp(db) = 10^(db/20). Handle amp=0.
concept: Decibels are logarithmic ratio: 20*log10 for amplitude, 10*log10 for power. +6dB ≈ double amplitude. +3dB ≈ double power. amp=0 → -∞ dB (digital silence). This is the scale every level meter, fader, and loudness specification uses.
audio_connection: Fundamental audio math. Every fader, meter, compressor, and limiter operates in dB. Know 0dBFS=max, -6dB=half amplitude, -96dB=16-bit noise floor.

title: Downsample Audio
problem: Downsample by factor N: moving average filter (anti-alias), then keep every Nth.
concept: Sample rate conversion requires anti-alias filtering BEFORE decimation (Nyquist). Without filtering, frequencies above new_rate/2 fold back as aliasing artifacts. Moving average is simplest low-pass filter. Real systems use higher-order filters. This is why you can't just 'skip samples' — you must filter first.
audio_connection: Sample rate conversion. 44.1kHz→16kHz for speech ML models. Anti-aliasing is mandatory per Nyquist theorem.
